1. Newborn Care: The First 28 Days

The neonatal period is critical for your baby’s health. Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal, a top neonatologist in Ajmer, shares key tips:

  • Breastfeeding: Exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months boosts immunity.
  • Umbilical Cord Care: Keep the area dry and clean to prevent infections.
  • Signs of Jaundice: Yellowish skin/eyes? Consult a child specialist in Ajmer immediately.

2. Vaccination Schedule: Don’t Miss These!

Dr. Goyal’s Mayur Childern Hospital follows the IAP-recommended immunization plan:

  • At Birth: BCG, Hepatitis B, OPV.
  • 6 Weeks: Pentavalent, Rotavirus, PCV.
  • 9-12 Months: MMR, Typhoid.

📌 Pro Tip: Book a vaccination consultation to avoid delays.

3. Nutrition for Growing Children

A balanced diet prevents childhood obesity and deficiencies. Dr. Goyal advises:

  • 0-6 Months: Only breast milk/formula.
  • 6+ Months: Introduce mashed fruits, cereals, and pulses.
  • Toddlers: Include proteins, dairy, and iron-rich foods.

4. Recognizing Pediatric Emergencies

When to rush to a 24/7 pediatric hospital in Ajmer:

  • High fever (>101°F) in infants under 3 months.
  • Severe dehydration (no tears, sunken eyes).
  • Breathing difficulties or seizures.

5. Developmental Milestones: Is Your Child on Track?

Regular growth monitoring in Ajmer helps detect delays early. Watch for:

  • By 6 Months: Rolls over, responds to sounds.
  • By 1 Year: Says “mama/dada,” stands with support.

title: Best Tips for Preventing Childhood Illnesses – Expert Guide by Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
heading: Best Tips for Preventing Childhood Illnesses
content:
Best Tips for Preventing Childhood Illnesses – Keep Your Child Healthy

As a parent, keeping your child safe from common illnesses is a big worry. Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal, a leading Pediatrician in Ajmer, shares the best tips to protect your child from infections, fevers, and other health problems. Read this guide to learn about symptoms, precautions, and when to visit a doctor.

1. Common Childhood Illnesses & Their Symptoms

Children often fall sick due to weak immunity. Here are some frequent illnesses and their signs:

  • Cold & Cough: Runny nose, sneezing, mild fever.
  • Stomach Infection: Vomiting, diarrhea, stomach pain.
  • Dengue/Fever: High temperature, body aches, rashes.
  • Chickenpox: Red spots, itching, fever.

🚨 Warning: If symptoms last more than 2-3 days, visit a child specialist in Ajmer immediately.

2. Best Ways to Prevent Illnesses in Children

Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal recommends these easy prevention tips:

  • Vaccinate on Time: Follow the vaccination schedule to protect against diseases.
  • Hand Washing: Teach kids to wash hands before eating and after playing.
  • Healthy Diet: Give fruits, vegetables, and milk to boost immunity.
  • Clean Surroundings: Keep toys, clothes, and home germ-free.

3. When to Visit a Pediatrician?

Some symptoms need a doctor’s attention. Rush to Mayur Childern Hospital if your child has:

  • 🔥 High Fever (Above 102°F) – Especially in babies under 1 year.
  • 💧 Dehydration Signs – Dry lips, no tears, less urination.
  • 🤒 Unusual Sleepiness – If the child is too weak or not responding.
  • 🤢 Severe Vomiting/Diarrhea – More than 5 times a day.

4. Home Care Tips for Sick Children

If your child has a mild illness, try these home remedies:

  • For Fever: Use a wet cloth on the forehead and give plenty of water.
  • For Cough: Warm honey (for kids above 1 year) and steam inhalation.
  • For Loose Motions: Give ORS solution and light food like khichdi.

⚠️ Note: If symptoms worsen, consult Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal.

5. Why Regular Checkups Are Important?

Visiting a child doctor in Ajmer every few months helps:

  • 📈 Track growth and development.
  • 💉 Stay updated on vaccinations.
  • 🩺 Detect hidden health issues early.

title: Seasonal Flu Infection in Children - Symptoms, Treatment & Prevention
heading: Seasonal Flu Infection in Children
content:
Seasonal Flu Infection in Children

Author: Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal

Hello, parents. I am Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal. At my child care center, I see many children with seasonal flu. When your child is sick, it can be scary. This blog will help you understand the flu in simple words.

What is the Seasonal Flu?

The flu is an illness caused by a virus. It is not just a simple cold. It can make your child feel very tired and sick. Children get the flu more easily because their bodies are still learning to fight germs.

What are the Signs of the Flu?

How can you tell if it is the flu? Look for these signs:

  • High Fever: The body becomes very hot quickly.
  • Body Pain: Your child may say their legs, arms, or back hurt.
  • Cough and Cold: Usually a dry cough and runny nose.
  • Feeling Very Tired: Your child may not want to play or eat.
  • Chills: They may feel cold and shiver.

How to Help Your Child at Home

Most children get better at home in a week. Here is what you can do:

  1. Let Them Rest: Sleep helps the body fight the virus.
  2. Give Lots of Water: Make sure they drink water, soup, or ORS. This stops them from getting dry (dehydrated).
  3. For Fever: You can give medicine like Paracetamol. Always ask your doctor for the right dose for your child's age and weight.
  4. Make Them Comfortable: A humidifier or steam can help with a blocked nose.

When Should You Call the Doctor?

Please call your doctor if you see:

  • Fast breathing or problems breathing.
  • Not drinking any water or milk.
  • Your child is too sleepy or won't wake up properly.
  • The fever goes away and comes back.

How to Stop the Flu (Prevention)

It is better to stop the flu than to treat it.

  1. Flu Shot (Vaccine): This is the best way to protect your child. We recommend flu shots for children above 6 months.
  2. Wash Hands: Teach your child to wash hands with soap before eating.
  3. Stay Away from Sick People: Try to avoid crowds when the flu is common.

Final Advice

Do not worry too much. The flu is common. With your love and care, most children get better. But if you are ever worried, please come see us. We are here to help.

Your child's health is our main goal.

title: Understanding Pneumonia in Children: Symptoms, Home Care & Prevention
heading: Understanding Pneumonia in Children
content:
Understanding Pneumonia in Children: A Guide for Parents

Hello parents. I am Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal. In my clinic, I meet many parents who are worried about pneumonia. I know this word can sound scary. But with good care, most children get well soon. This blog will help you understand pneumonia in simple words.

What is Pneumonia?

Pneumonia is a serious lung infection. The small air sacs in the lungs get filled with fluid or pus. This makes it hard for your child to breathe. It can be caused by germs like viruses or bacteria.

What are the Signs of Pneumonia?

It may start like a cold but gets worse. Look for these signs:

  • Bad Cough: Often with mucus (phlegm).
  • Fast Breathing: Your child's breathing may become quick. You may see their chest moving a lot.
  • Fever: Often a high fever.
  • Chest Pain: Especially when coughing.
  • Feeling Very Weak: Your child may not want to play at all.
  • No Hunger: They may not want to eat or drink.

How to Help Your Child at Home

If the doctor says it is okay to care for your child at home, you can do this:

  1. Let Them Rest: Sleep helps the body fight the infection.
  2. Give Lots of Fluids: Offer water, soup, or ORS. This keeps them hydrated and loosens the mucus.
  3. For Fever: You can give medicine like Paracetamol. Always use the dose your doctor told you for your child's age and weight.
  4. Use a Humidifier: This adds moisture to the air and can help with coughing.

Very Important: If the doctor gave antibiotics, make sure your child takes all of it. Do not stop even if they feel better.

When Should You Call the Doctor Immediately?

Call your doctor right away or go to the hospital if you see:

  • Big trouble breathing. The skin between their ribs gets sucked in with each breath.
  • Lips or nails turn blue.
  • A very high fever that won't go down.
  • Your child is too sleepy and hard to wake up.
  • No wet diapers or urine for many hours (dehydration).

How to Stop Pneumonia (Prevention)

It is better to prevent sickness than to treat it.

  1. Vaccines (Shots): This is the best protection. Make sure your child gets all their needed shots on time.
  2. Wash Hands: Teach your child to wash hands with soap often.
  3. Healthy Food: Good food helps build a strong body to fight germs.
  4. Clean Air: Keep your child away from smoke and dust.

Final Advice

Parents, please do not panic. Pneumonia is serious, but it can be treated. Your love and care are very important for your child's recovery. If you are ever worried, please come see me. I am here to help.

Your child's health is our main goal.

title: Understanding Your Child's Skin Rashes: A Pediatrician's Guide for Parents
heading: Understanding Your Child's Skin Rashes
content:
Understanding Your Child's Skin Rashes: A Pediatrician's Guide for Parents

Seeing rashes on your child's skin? Don't worry. Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal, a child specialist, explains the common causes, symptoms, and when you should see a doctor.

Hello, I am Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal. I am a Pediatrician and Neonatologist. Every day, I meet many worried parents who get scared when they see a rash on their child's skin.

I have only one request for you: "Please don't panic!"

A child's skin is very soft and sensitive. Getting rashes is very common. Most of the time, these rashes go away on their own or with simple care. Today, I will explain the reasons for these rashes and what you should do.

1. Why Do Rashes Happen?

There are many reasons for rashes in children. Here are the most common ones:

  • Infections: Viral fevers, chickenpox, measles, dengue, or fungal infections.
  • Allergies: From new foods, medicines, soap, lotion, or detergent used for washing clothes.
  • Heat or Sweat: Prickly heat (ghamoriya) or diaper rash.
  • Skin Problems: Like eczema.

2. How to Understand the Rash?

Rashes can look different. Please notice these things:

  • Type of Rash: Are they small red spots, water-filled blisters, or big patches?
  • Location: Where is the rash? Is it all over the body, only on the stomach, or near the joints?
  • Fever: Does your child have a fever? Did the fever come before or after the rash?
  • Itching: Is the child restless and trying to scratch the rash?
  • Colour: What is the colour of the rash? Red, pink, or white?

Knowing the answers to these questions helps me understand the problem better.

3. What Can You Do at Home?

If the rash is mild and your child is otherwise active and healthy, you can try these tips:

  • Dress your child in loose, soft, and cotton clothes.
  • Keep the skin clean and dry. Avoid very hot water for bathing.
  • For itching, you can use an ice pack. You can also give an anti-allergy medicine suggested by your doctor.
  • Do not use any new soap, lotion, or food for the child.
  • Keep your child hydrated. Give them plenty of water and fluids.

4. When Should You See a Doctor?

Most rashes are not dangerous. But, you must contact a doctor immediately if you see any of these signs:

  • High fever (more than 101°F) with the rash.
  • The rash is spreading very fast all over the body.
  • The rash is purple in colour or if there is bleeding from the rash.
  • The child is very weak, vomiting, or has difficulty breathing.
  • The itching is very severe and the child cannot sleep.

It is natural for parents to worry in such situations. But remember, getting a doctor's advice at the right time is the best solution.

5. Final Words

As a parent, I understand your worry. But with a little knowledge and the right steps, you can help your child feel better soon. When you see a rash, first stay calm. Try to understand the symptoms. If you see any serious signs, contact your doctor without delay.

If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact me. I am here for your child's good health.

Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
Pediatrician & Neonatologist

title: Taking Care of Your Newborn Baby: Simple Tips for New Parents
heading: Taking Care of Your Newborn Baby
content:
Taking Care of Your Newborn Baby: Simple Tips for New Parents

Hello, I am Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal. I am a child doctor. I help new parents take care of their babies every day.

I know having a new baby can feel scary. You might have many questions. That's normal! Today, I will share simple tips for newborn care.

1. First Few Days with Your Baby

Your baby is new to this world. Here's what you might see:

  • Sleep: Babies sleep most of the time. They wake up every few hours for feeding.
  • Umbilical Cord: Keep the belly button area clean and dry. It will fall off in 1-2 weeks.
  • Dirty Diapers: First poops are black and sticky. Later, they become yellow and soft. Learn more about newborn poop colors.
  • Crying: Baby cries when hungry, wet, or wants to be held. Learn how to decode your baby's cry.

2. Feeding Your Baby

  • Breastfeeding: Feed every 2-3 hours. Make sure baby latches properly.
  • Formula Milk: If using formula, make it exactly as written on the pack. Keep bottles clean.
  • Burping: After feeding, pat baby's back gently until they burp. This helps remove gas.
  • Wondering if your baby is getting enough food? Check our feeding guide.

3. Sleep Time

  • Safe Sleep: Always put baby to sleep on their back.
  • Bed: Use a firm mattress. No pillows, soft toys, or heavy blankets.
  • Your Room: Keep baby's bed in your room for first 6 months.
  • Learn about ideal room temperature for newborns.

4. Bath and Skin Care

  • Bathing: Give only sponge baths until the umbilical cord falls off. See our first bath guide.
  • Water: Use warm water. Check with your elbow - it should not feel too hot.
  • Soap: Use very mild, baby soap. Don't use too much.
  • Dry Well: Gently pat baby dry with soft towel.
  • Dealing with diaper rash? We can help.

5. When to Call Doctor

Call your doctor if you see:

  • Fever (more than 100.4°F)
  • Baby not feeding well
  • No wet diapers for 6-8 hours
  • Skin or eyes looking yellow
  • Baby too sleepy or crying too much
  • Breathing problems

Don't wait if you feel something is wrong. It's better to ask your doctor. For urgent concerns, visit our pediatric emergency services.

Final Words

Dear parents, you are doing great! Trust yourself. Don't be too hard on yourself. Enjoy these special moments with your baby. They grow up very fast!

If you need any help, I am always here for you and your baby. Explore more newborn care resources on our website.

Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
Child Specialist & Newborn Doctor

title: Is Your Newborn Baby Sick? 7 Danger Signs Every Parent Must Know
heading: 7 Newborn Danger Signs Every Parent Must Know
content:
Is Your Newborn Baby Sick? 7 Danger Signs Every Parent Must Know

Hello, parents. I am Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal. I am a child doctor in Ajmer. I see many newborn babies and their parents in my clinic.

I know you love your baby very much. When your baby is sick, you feel very worried. This is normal. Today, I will tell you about 7 important danger signs. If you see these signs, you must call your child doctor in Ajmer quickly.

1. High Fever

  • What to see: If your baby feels very hot.
  • What to do: Use a thermometer. If the temperature is 100.4°F or more, it is dangerous for a small baby. Do not give any medicine. Call your doctor now.

2. Breathing Problems

Look for these signs:

  • Fast Breathing: Baby's chest is moving up and down very fast.
  • Noisy Breathing: You hear a strange sound when the baby breathes.
  • Hard to Breathe: Baby is using too much force to breathe. You can see the skin between ribs getting pulled in.

If you notice severe breathing difficulties, this may require emergency pediatric services.

3. Baby is Too Weak or Too Sleepy

  • Not Drinking Milk: Baby is not interested in feeding. Or baby is too weak to suck milk properly.
  • Not Waking Up: Baby is sleeping too much and is very difficult to wake up. Baby feels limp like a rag doll.

4. Not Peeing Enough (Dehydration)

  • Check Diaper: If there are less than 6 wet diapers in one day.
  • Dry Mouth: Look inside baby's mouth. It should be wet. If it is dry, it is a bad sign.

For more guidance on newborn health, visit our newborn care section.

5. Yellow Colour (Jaundice)

  • What to see: The white part of baby's eyes turns yellow. Baby's skin also can turn yellow.
  • When to worry: If the yellow colour is increasing and the baby is also not feeding well and is very sleepy.

Jaundice is one of the common newborn conditions we treat at our clinic.

6. Bad Vomiting

All babies spit up a little milk. But be careful if:

  • The vomit is green in colour.
  • Vomiting is very strong and shoots out far.

7. Blue Colour

  • Where to see: Look at baby's lips and tongue.
  • What it means: If they turn blue or grey, it means baby is not getting enough air. This is very serious.

This is a critical condition that requires immediate pediatric critical care.

My Final Advice

Dear parents, you know your baby best. If you feel something is wrong, please do not wait. It is always better to call your doctor and ask.

I am here to help you. As your child doctor in Ajmer, you can always call me if you are worried about your newborn baby. For comprehensive care, explore our neonatology services.

Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
Consultant Pediatrician & Neonatologist

title: Diarrhoea in Children: A Complete Guide for Parents
heading: Diarrhoea in Children: A Complete Guide for Parents
content:
Diarrhoea in Children: A Complete Guide for Parents

Hello, dear parents. I am Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal, child specialist in Ajmer. At my clinic, Mayur Childern Hospital, one of the most common problems I see in children is diarrhoea, which we often call loose motions.

As a parent, it is heartbreaking to see your child unwell and unable to tell you what exactly is wrong. I see your worry, and I want to help. This guide will explain everything you need to know about diarrhoea in a simple way.

What Exactly is Diarrhoea?

Diarrhoea means passing loose or watery stools three or more times a day. It happens when germs (like viruses or bacteria) irritate your child's stomach and intestines. While it is the body's way of getting rid of the germs, it also causes the loss of vital water and salts from the body.

Why is Diarrhoea Dangerous for Children?

The biggest and most serious problem diarrhoea can cause is dehydration. This means your child's body loses more fluid than it takes in. Young children can become dehydrated very quickly, which can become serious if not treated.

How to Spot Dehydration: Important Signs for Parents

You are the first person who can see if your child is getting dehydrated. Please look for these signs:

  • Passing less urine: Fewer wet nappies than usual. For a baby, less than 6 wet nappies in 24 hours is a warning sign. For an older child, if they don't pass urine for 6-8 hours, be alert.
  • Dry mouth and tongue: The inside of the mouth will look and feel dry, not wet and shiny.
  • No tears while crying: When your child cries, especially a baby, no tears come out.
  • Sunken eyes: The eyes may look deep and have dark circles.
  • Unusual behaviour: Your child may become very tired, weak, drowsy, or extremely irritable and fussy.
  • Cold hands and feet: The skin may feel cool to the touch.

Dr. Mayur's Home Care Advice: What to Do When Your Child Has Diarrhoea

For mild cases, you can successfully care for your child at home by following these steps:

  1. Focus on Fluids, Not Food: The most important thing is to keep your child hydrated.
    • For Babies: Please continue breastfeeding or formula feeding. You can also give Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) between feeds. Breast milk is the best medicine.
    • For Older Children: Give small sips of ORS frequently. You can also offer buttermilk (chaas), lentil water (dal ka pani), or coconut water. ORS is the best as it replaces the lost salts perfectly.
  2. Offer Gentle Foods: Once your child is taking fluids well, you can offer small amounts of easy-to-digest food.
    • Good choices are khichdi, plain rice with curd, banana, apple sauce, or boiled potatoes.
    • Remember the new rule: "Feed a gut infection." Giving food helps the intestine heal.
  3. Keep Them Clean and Comfortable:
    • Let your child rest as much as possible.
    • If your child wears diapers, change them immediately after every loose stool. Use a good barrier cream like zinc oxide to prevent a painful nappy rash. For persistent issues, learn about diaper rash treatment.

When You Must Call Your Child Doctor in Ajmer: The Danger Signs

Please do not wait if you see any of the following signs. Call me immediately.

  • You see signs of dehydration (as listed above).
  • Blood or mucus in the stool.
  • High fever that does not come down.
  • Frequent vomiting and inability to keep any liquids down.
  • Severe stomach pain that doesn't go away.
  • Your child becomes very lethargic, floppy, or difficult to wake up.

For urgent cases, don't hesitate to visit our emergency services.

What You Should Avoid Doing

  • Do not give sugary drinks like cola, packaged fruit juices, or sweetened sports drinks. They can make the diarrhoea worse.
  • Do not give any adult anti-diarrhoea medicines to your child. They can be dangerous for children.
  • Do not stop breastfeeding or formula milk.

My Final Words to You

Dear parents, I know how anxious you feel when your child is sick. My best advice is to trust your instincts. You know your child better than anyone else. If you feel that something is not right, even if you are not sure, please do not hesitate. It is always better to be safe and call your doctor. A quick conversation can ease your worry and set you on the right path.

My door and phone are always open for you. Let's work together to keep your little ones healthy and happy.

With warm regards,

Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
Consultant Pediatrician & Neonatologist

title: Urine Tract Infection (UTI) in Children: A Simple Guide for Parents
heading: Urine Tract Infection (UTI) in Children: A Simple Guide for Parents
content:
Urine Tract Infection (UTI) in Children: A Simple Guide for Parents

Hello, dear parents. I am Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal, your child specialist in Ajmer. At Mayur Childern Hospital, I often meet worried parents whose children are suffering from a Urine Tract Infection, or UTI.

It can be confusing when a young child has a fever or seems uncomfortable but can't tell you where it hurts. I understand your concern. This guide will help you understand what a UTI is and what you can do.

What is a Urine Tract Infection (UTI)?

A UTI is an infection in any part of the system that handles our urine (pee). This includes the kidneys, the tubes connecting them (ureters), the bladder, and the tube that passes urine out of the body (urethra). It happens when germs, usually bacteria, get into this system and cause an infection.

Why Do Children Get UTIs?

Germs from the skin or stool can sometimes find their way into the urinary tract. This is more common in girls because their urethra is shorter and closer to the anus. In babies, wearing a soiled diaper for too long can also sometimes increase the risk.

How to Spot a UTI: Important Signs for Parents

It can be tricky, especially with young children who can't talk. Please look for these signs:

  • Fever: Often the only sign in a baby, especially if it has no clear cause like a cold or cough.
  • Pain or Burning: An older child may complain of pain or a burning feeling when they urinate.
  • Stomach Pain or Discomfort: Your child may have pain in the lower belly or back.
  • Urgent Need to Go: They may feel a sudden, strong need to urinate, often passing only a few drops.
  • Going Too Often: Needing to urinate much more often than usual.
  • Bedwetting: A child who is usually dry at night might start wetting the bed again.
  • Foul-Smelling or Cloudy Urine: The urine may look cloudy or smell bad.
  • Being Fussy: Babies might be unusually irritable, cry during nappy changes, or feed poorly.

Dr. Mayur's Advice: What to Do If You Suspect a UTI

If you notice any of the signs above, here is what you should do:

  1. See a Doctor: The most important step is to bring your child to the clinic. UTIs need proper medical diagnosis and treatment with antibiotics prescribed by a doctor.
  2. Get a Urine Test: We will need a small sample of your child's urine to confirm the infection. I will guide you on how to collect it correctly.
  3. Give the Full Course of Medicine: If your child is prescribed antibiotics, it is very important to finish the entire course, even if they start feeling better. This ensures the infection is completely gone.
  4. Offer Plenty of Fluids: Encourage your child to drink more water. This helps flush out the bacteria from their system.

When You Must Call Your Child Doctor in Ajmer

Please do not wait if you see any of the following signs. Call me immediately.

  • Your baby under 3 months has a fever.
  • Your child has a high fever with chills.
  • They have severe pain in the back or stomach.
  • They are vomiting and cannot keep medicine down.
  • They seem very weak, drowsy, or difficult to wake up.

For urgent cases, don't hesitate to visit our emergency services.

What You Should Avoid Doing

  • Do not give leftover antibiotics from an old illness.
  • Do not ignore the symptoms, thinking they will go away on their own.
  • Do not give sugary drinks or cola; water is the best choice.

My Final Words to You

Dear parents, a UTI is a common childhood illness that can be treated effectively. The key is to notice the signs early and get the right treatment. As always, trust your instincts. You know your child best. If you feel something is wrong, please reach out. A quick check-up can give you peace of mind and get your child back to good health.

My door and phone are always open for you. Let's work together to keep your little ones healthy and happy.

With warm regards,

Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
Consultant Pediatrician & Neonatologist

title: Infantile Colic: Understanding Why Your Baby Won't Stop Crying
heading: Infantile Colic: Understanding Why Your Baby Won't Stop Crying
content:
Your Baby Won't Stop Crying? Let's Understand Colic Together

A Quick Note from Dr. Mayur:

Hi there, I'm Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal. In my clinic, Mayur Childern Hospital in Ajmer, I meet many tired and worried parents with the same story: "Doctor, my healthy baby cries for hours, and we can't calm him down."

If you feel this way, you are not alone. Let's talk about what this might be and what you can do.

What is Colic, Really?

Think of colic as a time when a healthy baby cries a lot for no clear reason.

It's like a daily puzzle. The crying often happens in the evening and can feel unstoppable. The good news? It is temporary and usually goes away on its own in a few months.

Spotting the Signs: Is it Colic?

Here's what you might see in a baby with colic:

  • Long Crying Spells: Intense crying that can last for 1-3 hours, often at the same time every day.
  • Body Clues: They may pull their legs up to their tummy, clench their fists, or their face may turn red.
  • Hard to Comfort: Your usual tricks like feeding or rocking don't seem to work during this time.

Soothing Your Baby: What Can You Try?

There is no single magic solution, but you can try these simple ideas:

1. Comforting Touch:

  • Gently rub your baby's back.
  • Hold them against your chest so they can hear your heartbeat.
  • Try a warm (not hot) water bottle on their tummy over their clothes.

2. Gentle Motion:

  • Rock them gently in your arms.
  • Take them for a slow walk in a stroller.
  • A calm car ride can sometimes work wonders.

3. Calming Sounds:

  • Sing a soft lullaby or play gentle music.
  • The steady hum of a fan or washing machine can be soothing.

4. Feeding Check:

  • If you bottle-feed, make sure your baby isn't swallowing too much air. Try a different bottle if needed.
  • Always burp your baby well after every feed. For more guidance on newborn feeding, visit our resources.

A Very Important Message for YOU, the Parent

Listening to a crying baby for hours is hard. It can make you feel stressed and helpless.

Please remember: It is okay to take a break.

If you feel overwhelmed, place your baby safely in their crib and step away for a few minutes. Take some deep breaths. Ask your partner or a family member for help. Taking care of yourself is not selfish; it is necessary.

When Should You Call Your Doctor?

Colic is common, but it's important to be sure it's just colic. Please call me at my clinic if you see any of these signs:

  • Fever of 100.4ºF or more.
  • Vomiting (especially green or forceful vomiting).
  • Your baby is very weak, too sleepy, or difficult to wake up.
  • No wet diapers for over 6-8 hours - this could indicate dehydration.
  • Blood in the stool.
  • They are not gaining weight.

If you notice any of these danger signs, don't hesitate to visit our emergency services.

My Final Word to You

Dear parents, you are doing a great job. Colic is a phase, and it will pass. Trust your love and your instincts. If you are ever worried, I am just a phone call away. Let's keep your little one comfortable and your mind at peace.

For more information on newborn care or to learn about other pediatric services we offer, visit our website.

With warm wishes,

Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal

title: Dengue Fever in Children: A Simple Guide for Parents by Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
heading: Dengue Fever in Children: A Simple Guide for Parents
content:
Dengue Fever in Children: A Simple Guide for Parents

Hello, dear parents. I am Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal, your child specialist in Ajmer. At Mayur Childern Hospital, I often meet concerned parents during the monsoon and post-monsoon seasons when cases of Dengue fever are on the rise.

It can be frightening to see your child unwell with high fever and body pain, especially when you hear about Dengue in the news. I understand your worry. This guide will help you understand what Dengue is, how to recognize it, and what steps you should take to protect your child.

What is Dengue Fever?

Dengue is a viral illness spread by the bite of an infected Aedes mosquito. This mosquito usually bites during the daytime, especially in the early morning and late afternoon. Dengue cannot spread directly from one person to another.

Why Are Children More Vulnerable?

Children, especially younger ones, are more vulnerable because their immune systems are still developing. They often play outdoors, making them more exposed to mosquito bites if proper precautions are not taken.

How to Spot Dengue: Important Signs for Parents

The symptoms of Dengue can vary from mild to severe. Please watch for these signs:

  • High Fever: A sudden, high-grade fever (104°F or 40°C) is often the first sign. Learn more about managing fever in children.
  • Severe Body Aches: Your child may complain of severe pain in muscles and joints. This is why Dengue is sometimes called "breakbone fever."
  • Pain Behind the Eyes: This is a classic symptom of Dengue.
  • Severe Headache: Often accompanied by eye pain.
  • Skin Rash: A red rash may appear a few days after the fever starts. For more on skin rashes in children, read our detailed guide.
  • Nausea and Vomiting: Your child may feel nauseous or vomit frequently.
  • Mild Bleeding: Look for signs like nosebleeds, bleeding gums, or easy bruising.
  • Lethargy and Weakness: Your child may seem unusually tired, weak, or restless.

Dr. Mayur's Advice: What to Do If You Suspect Dengue

If you notice any of the signs above, here is what you should do:

  1. See a Doctor Immediately: Early diagnosis and management are crucial. Bring your child to the clinic for a proper evaluation. Book a consultation at our General Pediatrics OPD.
  2. Get a Blood Test: A simple blood test (like NS1 Antigen or Dengue Serology) can confirm the infection. I will advise you on the right test and timing.
  3. Keep Your Child Hydrated: Encourage plenty of fluids – water, oral rehydration solution (ORS), coconut water, or clear soups. Good hydration is the most important part of treatment. For dehydration concerns, read about dehydration in children.
  4. Manage Fever: Give only Paracetamol for fever and pain. Avoid medicines like Ibuprofen, Aspirin, or other NSAIDs, as they can increase the risk of bleeding.
  5. Ensure Rest: Let your child rest as much as possible to help their body recover.

When You Must Call Your Child Doctor in Ajmer: The Warning Signs

Dengue can become serious quickly. Please do not wait if you see any of the following warning signs. Call me immediately.

  • Severe abdominal pain or persistent vomiting.
  • Bleeding from the nose or gums, or blood in vomit or stool.
  • Your child is very lethargic, restless, or difficult to wake.
  • Cold, clammy hands and feet, or pale skin.
  • Difficulty in breathing.

For urgent cases, don't hesitate to visit our emergency services. If symptoms are severe, we also provide pediatric critical care.

What You Should Avoid Doing

  • Do not self-medicate. Avoid Aspirin and Ibuprofen.
  • Do not ignore warning signs, thinking it's just a viral fever.
  • Do not give dark-colored drinks or sodas. Stick to clear fluids.
  • Do not delay seeking medical advice.

Prevention is Key: Protect Your Child from Mosquitoes

The best way to fight Dengue is to prevent it. Especially during the Dengue season:

  • Prevent Mosquito Breeding: Empty stagnant water from coolers, flower pots, old tyres, and any containers around your home.
  • Use Mosquito Protection: Use mosquito nets, repellents, and dress your child in full-sleeved clothes when outdoors.
  • Keep Windows and Doors Screened: Install nets to prevent mosquitoes from entering your home.
  • Vaccinate: While there's no specific Dengue vaccine for all children yet, keeping up with general vaccination schedules helps maintain overall immunity.

For more tips on preventing childhood illnesses, visit our blog.

My Final Words to You

Dear parents, while Dengue can be serious, most children recover well with timely and proper medical care. The key is to be alert, especially during the Dengue season. As always, trust your instincts. You know your child best. If you feel something is not right, please reach out. A quick check-up can make all the difference.

My door and phone are always open for you. Let's work together to keep your little ones safe, healthy, and happy.

With warm regards,

Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
Consultant Pediatrician & Neonatologist

title: Healthy Eating Habits for Growing Kids | Expert Nutrition Advice By Dr Mayur Kumar Goyal
heading: Healthy Eating Habits for Growing Kids
content:
Healthy Eating Habits for Growing Kids | Expert Nutrition Advice

As parents, we all want our children to grow up healthy, active, and happy. One of the most important ways to support a child's growth and development is by encouraging healthy eating habits from an early age.

The foods children eat today play a major role in their physical growth, brain development, energy levels, and overall well-being. Good nutrition not only helps children perform better in school and sports but also lays the foundation for a healthier future.

Why Is Healthy Eating Important for Children?

Growing children need a variety of nutrients to support their rapidly developing bodies and minds. A balanced diet provides vitamins, minerals, proteins, carbohydrates, and healthy fats necessary for healthy growth.

  • Healthy growth and development
  • Strong bones and teeth
  • Better concentration and learning abilities
  • Improved immunity
  • Higher energy levels
  • Healthy weight management
  • Better overall physical and mental health

What Makes a Healthy Diet for Kids?

1. Fill Their Plate with Fruits and Vegetables

Fruits and vegetables are packed with vitamins, minerals, fiber, and antioxidants that support healthy growth and immunity.

  • Apples
  • Bananas
  • Oranges
  • Berries
  • Carrots
  • Spinach
  • Broccoli
  • Tomatoes
  • Sweet potatoes
  • Bell peppers

2. Choose Whole Grains Whenever Possible

Whole grains provide long-lasting energy and support healthy digestion.

  • Whole wheat bread
  • Brown rice
  • Oats
  • Whole grain cereals
  • Quinoa

3. Include Protein in Every Meal

Protein is essential for muscle development and overall growth.

  • Eggs
  • Chicken
  • Fish
  • Lean meats
  • Beans
  • Lentils
  • Nuts and seeds
  • Dairy products

4. Don't Forget Calcium-Rich Foods

Childhood is the perfect time to build strong bones and teeth.

  • Milk
  • Yogurt
  • Cheese
  • Fortified plant-based milk
  • Green leafy vegetables

5. Include Healthy Fats

Healthy fats are important for brain development and overall growth.

  • Avocados
  • Nuts
  • Seeds
  • Olive oil
  • Fatty fish such as salmon

Why Regular Meal Times Matter

Children thrive on routines. Having regular meal and snack times helps regulate appetite and encourages healthier eating patterns.

  • A healthy breakfast every day
  • Balanced lunch and dinner meals
  • Nutritious snacks when needed
  • Family meals whenever possible

Healthy Snack Ideas for Kids

  • Fresh fruit slices
  • Yogurt with berries
  • Whole grain crackers
  • Vegetable sticks with hummus
  • Cheese cubes
  • Homemade smoothies
  • Roasted nuts (for older children)

Try to limit chips, candy, sugary drinks, packaged snacks, and highly processed foods.

How Can Parents Encourage Healthy Eating Habits?

  • Be a positive role model.
  • Involve children in meal preparation.
  • Offer a variety of healthy foods.
  • Avoid using food as a reward.
  • Make mealtimes enjoyable.

Limiting Sugary and Processed Foods

Too much sugar can increase the risk of obesity, tooth decay, and other health issues. Water should be the primary beverage choice for most children.

What If My Child Is a Picky Eater?

Picky eating is common during childhood. Introduce new foods gradually, serve small portions, avoid pressuring your child, and remain patient and consistent.

The Importance of Family Meals

Children who regularly eat meals with their families are more likely to eat more fruits and vegetables, develop healthier eating habits, improve communication skills, and experience better emotional well-being.

Nutrition and Physical Activity Go Hand in Hand

Encourage children to play outdoors, participate in sports, ride bicycles, walk regularly, and limit screen time.

When Should You Seek Professional Advice?

Consult a pediatrician if you notice poor growth, extreme picky eating, food allergies, nutritional deficiencies, obesity concerns, or underweight issues.

Final Thoughts

Healthy eating habits developed during childhood often last a lifetime. By offering balanced meals, encouraging nutritious food choices, and creating positive mealtime experiences, parents can help children build a healthy relationship with food and ensure a healthier future.

title: Acute Gastroenteritis in Children: Symptoms, Treatment & Prevention | Expert Advice By Dr Mayur Kumar Goyal
heading: Acute Gastroenteritis in Children: Symptoms, Treatment & Prevention
content:
Acute Gastroenteritis in Children: Symptoms, Treatment & Prevention

As a parent, seeing your child suffer from vomiting, diarrhea, or stomach pain can be worrying. One of the most common reasons behind these symptoms is acute gastroenteritis, often called a stomach infection or stomach flu.

Although most cases improve within a few days, gastroenteritis can sometimes lead to severe dehydration, especially in young children. Understanding its symptoms, causes, treatment options, and prevention tips can help parents manage the condition effectively and know when to seek medical attention.

What is Acute Gastroenteritis?

Acute gastroenteritis is a sudden inflammation of the stomach and intestines caused by infections or contaminated food and water. It commonly results in diarrhea, vomiting, stomach cramps, and fever.

Children are more vulnerable to gastroenteritis because their immune systems are still developing, and the infection can spread quickly in schools, daycare centers, playgrounds, and even within families.

What Causes Gastroenteritis in Children?

1. Viral Infections

Viruses are the most common cause of gastroenteritis in children.

  • Rotavirus
  • Norovirus
  • Adenovirus

2. Bacterial Infections

Bacteria can enter the body through contaminated food and water.

  • E. coli
  • Salmonella
  • Shigella
  • Campylobacter

3. Contaminated Food and Water

Eating unhygienic food or drinking unsafe water significantly increases the risk of infection.

4. Poor Hand Hygiene

Children often forget to wash their hands properly before eating or after using the toilet, making it easier for germs to spread.

5. Contact with Infected Individuals

Gastroenteritis is highly contagious and can spread through close contact with infected individuals.

What Are the Symptoms of Acute Gastroenteritis?

  • Frequent loose stools or diarrhea
  • Vomiting
  • Fever
  • Stomach cramps
  • Abdominal pain
  • Nausea
  • Loss of appetite
  • Weakness and fatigue
  • Irritability in infants and toddlers

Signs of Dehydration Parents Should Never Ignore

  • Dry mouth and cracked lips
  • Reduced urination
  • Dark yellow urine
  • No tears while crying
  • Sunken eyes
  • Excessive sleepiness
  • Dizziness
  • Cold hands and feet
  • Rapid heartbeat

When Should You Take Your Child to a Doctor?

Contact your doctor immediately if your child experiences:

  • Persistent vomiting
  • Blood in stool or vomit
  • High fever
  • Severe stomach pain
  • Refusal to drink fluids
  • Signs of dehydration
  • Difficulty breathing
  • Seizures
  • Diarrhea lasting longer than three days

How is Gastroenteritis Diagnosed?

Doctors usually diagnose gastroenteritis based on symptoms and physical examination. In severe cases, additional tests such as stool examination, blood tests, urine tests, and electrolyte assessment may be recommended.

Treatment of Acute Gastroenteritis in Children

1. Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS)

ORS is the most effective treatment for preventing dehydration and replacing lost fluids and electrolytes.

2. Encourage Plenty of Fluids

  • Water
  • Coconut water
  • Clear soups
  • Breast milk for infants

Avoid soft drinks, packaged fruit juices, energy drinks, and caffeinated beverages.

3. Continue Feeding Your Child

Recommended foods during recovery include:

  • Rice
  • Khichdi
  • Bananas
  • Toast
  • Applesauce
  • Boiled potatoes
  • Curd or yogurt

Foods to avoid include spicy foods, fried foods, oily snacks, junk food, and excess sugary foods.

4. Medications

Depending on the condition, doctors may prescribe zinc supplements, probiotics, fever-reducing medicines, and antibiotics when bacterial infection is confirmed.

5. Hospital Treatment

Severe dehydration may require hospitalization and treatment with intravenous fluids and electrolyte correction.

How Can Gastroenteritis Be Prevented?

  • Wash hands regularly.
  • Drink safe, filtered, or boiled water.
  • Avoid unhygienic food.
  • Keep surroundings clean.
  • Ensure rotavirus vaccination.
  • Keep sick children at home until they recover.

Possible Complications of Untreated Gastroenteritis

  • Severe dehydration
  • Electrolyte imbalance
  • Kidney problems
  • Malnutrition
  • Extreme weakness
  • Hospitalization

How Long Does Recovery Take?

Most children recover within 2 to 5 days. During recovery, focus on adequate hydration, nutritious meals, and sufficient rest to help children regain their strength quickly.

Final Thoughts

Acute gastroenteritis is one of the most common childhood illnesses, especially during the summer and rainy seasons. Symptoms such as diarrhea, vomiting, stomach pain, and fever can be distressing, but most children recover well with proper hydration and supportive care. Timely use of ORS, safe drinking water, proper hygiene, and medical care when needed can help ensure a smooth and speedy recovery.

title: Pneumonia: Symptoms, Causes, Treatment & Prevention | Expert Advice By Dr Mayur Kumar Goyal
heading: Pneumonia: Symptoms, Causes, Treatment & Prevention
content:
Pneumonia: Symptoms, Causes, Treatment & Prevention

Pneumonia is a common yet serious lung infection that affects millions of people every year. While some cases are mild and recover with proper treatment, others can become life-threatening if ignored. Young children, older adults, and people with weak immune systems are especially at risk.

If you've ever wondered what pneumonia is, how it develops, or when you should seek medical help, this guide will help you understand everything in simple terms.

What is Pneumonia?

Pneumonia is an infection that causes inflammation in one or both lungs. Inside the lungs are tiny air sacs called alveoli that help exchange oxygen. When pneumonia occurs, these air sacs fill with fluid or pus, making it difficult for the lungs to function properly.

As a result, patients may experience coughing, fever, chest pain, and difficulty breathing.

What Are the Common Symptoms of Pneumonia?

  • Persistent cough
  • Fever and chills
  • Shortness of breath
  • Chest pain while breathing or coughing
  • Rapid breathing
  • Fatigue and weakness
  • Excessive sweating
  • Loss of appetite
  • Nausea or vomiting
  • Confusion, especially in older adults

In children, symptoms may include irritability, poor feeding, and unusually fast breathing.

What Causes Pneumonia?

1. Bacterial Pneumonia

Bacterial pneumonia is often caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae bacteria and may lead to high fever, productive cough, chest pain, and severe weakness.

2. Viral Pneumonia

Viruses such as influenza, RSV, and coronavirus can also cause pneumonia and often begin with flu-like symptoms.

3. Fungal Pneumonia

Fungal pneumonia is less common and usually affects individuals with weakened immune systems.

4. Aspiration Pneumonia

This occurs when food, saliva, liquids, or vomit accidentally enter the lungs, leading to infection and inflammation.

Who Is More Likely to Develop Pneumonia?

  • Infants and young children
  • Adults over 65 years of age
  • Smokers
  • People with asthma or COPD
  • Diabetic patients
  • Individuals with heart disease
  • People with weakened immunity
  • Patients recovering from viral infections

Poor nutrition, air pollution, and crowded living conditions can also increase the risk.

Different Types of Pneumonia

  • Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) – Occurs outside hospitals or healthcare facilities.
  • Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (HAP) – Develops during hospitalization and can be more serious.
  • Walking Pneumonia – A milder form where patients often continue their daily activities.
  • Severe Pneumonia – May require emergency treatment and intensive care support.

How Does Pneumonia Spread?

  • Coughing and sneezing
  • Close contact with infected individuals
  • Contaminated surfaces
  • Airborne respiratory droplets

How Is Pneumonia Diagnosed?

If pneumonia is suspected, doctors may recommend:

  • Chest X-ray
  • Blood tests
  • Sputum examination
  • Pulse oximetry
  • CT scan in severe cases

Treatment Options for Pneumonia

Antibiotics

Bacterial pneumonia is usually treated with antibiotics. Completing the full course is essential.

Antiviral Medications

Certain viral infections may require antiviral treatment.

Medicines for Fever and Pain

Doctors may prescribe medicines to reduce fever, body aches, and chest discomfort.

Oxygen Therapy

Patients experiencing low oxygen levels or breathing difficulties may require oxygen support.

Hospitalization

Hospital admission may be necessary for elderly patients, young children, and individuals with severe symptoms.

Home Care Tips for Faster Recovery

  • Drink plenty of fluids
  • Get adequate rest
  • Avoid smoking
  • Eat healthy, nutritious meals
  • Complete all prescribed medications
  • Use a humidifier if advised by your doctor
  • Avoid strenuous activities until fully recovered

What Happens If Pneumonia Is Left Untreated?

  • Lung abscess
  • Respiratory failure
  • Sepsis (blood infection)
  • Fluid accumulation around the lungs
  • Organ failure

In severe cases, untreated pneumonia can become life-threatening.

Pneumonia in Children

Children are more vulnerable because their immune systems are still developing.

  • Rapid breathing
  • Wheezing
  • Fever
  • Poor feeding
  • Bluish lips
  • Persistent crying

Parents should seek immediate medical attention if a child develops breathing difficulties.

Pneumonia in Older Adults

Older adults may experience confusion, extreme weakness, drowsiness, and reduced appetite rather than high fever.

Can Pneumonia Be Prevented?

  • Get vaccinated against pneumococcal disease, influenza, and COVID-19.
  • Practice good hand hygiene.
  • Avoid smoking.
  • Eat a balanced diet.
  • Avoid close contact with sick individuals.
  • Cover your mouth while coughing or sneezing.

When Should You See a Doctor?

  • Difficulty breathing
  • High fever
  • Persistent chest pain
  • Bluish lips or fingernails
  • Severe weakness
  • Confusion

Early diagnosis and treatment greatly improve recovery outcomes.

Final Thoughts

Pneumonia is a serious lung infection that should never be ignored. Symptoms such as persistent cough, fever, chest pain, and breathing difficulties require timely medical attention. The good news is that with early diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and proper care, most patients recover completely. Staying vaccinated, maintaining good hygiene, eating a healthy diet, and avoiding smoking can significantly reduce your risk of developing pneumonia.

title: Dengue, Malaria & Chikungunya in Children: Warning Signs Parents Should Never Ignore | Expert Advice By Dr Mayur Kumar Goyal
heading: Dengue, Malaria & Chikungunya in Children: Warning Signs Parents Should Never Ignore
content:
Dengue, Malaria & Chikungunya in Children: Warning Signs Parents Should Never Ignore

Monsoon brings welcome relief from the summer heat, but it also increases the risk of mosquito-borne diseases such as dengue, malaria, and chikungunya. Children are particularly vulnerable because their immune systems are still developing, making them more susceptible to infections and complications. While these illnesses often begin with common symptoms like fever, they can quickly become serious if left untreated.

As a parent, understanding the early warning signs and seeking timely medical care can make a significant difference in your child's recovery. Knowing what to watch for helps ensure that your child receives the right treatment before the condition worsens.

Understanding Dengue in Children

Dengue is a viral infection transmitted through the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes, which usually bite during the daytime. It commonly occurs during the rainy season due to stagnant water that serves as breeding grounds for mosquitoes.

Children with dengue typically develop a sudden high fever that may last for several days. Along with fever, they may complain of severe headache, pain behind the eyes, muscle and joint pain, nausea, vomiting, loss of appetite, and skin rashes. Although many children recover with proper hydration and medical supervision, some may develop severe dengue, which requires immediate hospitalization.

Parents should never ignore symptoms such as persistent vomiting, severe abdominal pain, unusual sleepiness, difficulty breathing, bleeding from the gums or nose, blood in vomit or stool, or cold and clammy hands and feet. These signs may indicate that the disease is progressing to a more dangerous stage and needs urgent medical attention.

Understanding Malaria in Children

Malaria is caused by parasites transmitted through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. Unlike dengue, malaria often causes recurring episodes of fever accompanied by chills and sweating.

A child suffering from malaria may experience high fever, shaking chills, excessive sweating, weakness, headache, body aches, vomiting, poor appetite, and fatigue. In younger children, symptoms may be less specific and include irritability, drowsiness, refusal to eat, or excessive crying.

If malaria is not diagnosed and treated promptly, it can lead to serious complications such as severe anemia, seizures, breathing difficulties, and damage to vital organs. Parents should seek immediate medical evaluation whenever a child develops repeated fever with chills, especially during the monsoon season or after mosquito exposure.

Understanding Chikungunya in Children

Chikungunya is another viral disease spread by Aedes mosquitoes. Although it is generally less dangerous than severe dengue, it can cause considerable discomfort and prolonged joint pain.

Children with chikungunya often develop sudden fever accompanied by severe joint pain. They may also experience muscle pain, headache, skin rash, swelling around the joints, tiredness, and reduced physical activity. While most children recover within one to two weeks, joint pain may continue for several weeks in some cases.

Although complications are uncommon, persistent fever, dehydration, excessive weakness, confusion, or difficulty walking should never be ignored and require prompt medical assessment.

How to Differentiate Between Dengue, Malaria, and Chikungunya

The symptoms of these three diseases often overlap, making it difficult for parents to identify the exact illness at home. Dengue usually presents with high fever, body pain, rash, and low platelet counts. Malaria is more commonly associated with fever cycles, chills, and sweating. Chikungunya is characterized by severe joint pain that may continue even after the fever subsides.

Since the symptoms are similar, laboratory investigations are essential for an accurate diagnosis. Doctors may recommend blood tests to identify the specific infection and determine the appropriate treatment. Self-diagnosis or home treatment without medical advice may delay proper care and increase the risk of complications.

Warning Signs Parents Should Never Ignore

Every fever during the rainy season deserves careful attention, especially in children. Certain symptoms indicate that the illness may be becoming severe and require immediate medical evaluation.

If your child has persistent high fever lasting more than two days, repeated vomiting, severe abdominal pain, excessive sleepiness, confusion, breathing difficulty, inability to drink fluids, reduced urine output, seizures, bleeding from the nose or gums, black stools, blood in vomit, severe weakness, or becomes unusually irritable or unresponsive, seek emergency medical care without delay.

Early medical intervention can prevent serious complications and significantly improve recovery outcomes.

Diagnosis and Treatment

Doctors diagnose these infections based on symptoms, physical examination, and specific blood tests. Dengue is confirmed through NS1 antigen tests, IgM antibody tests, or other laboratory investigations. Malaria is diagnosed using blood smear examination or rapid malaria antigen tests. Chikungunya is confirmed through antibody testing or PCR tests in certain cases.

Treatment depends on the specific disease. Dengue primarily requires supportive care, including adequate hydration, fever management, and close monitoring of platelet counts and warning signs. Malaria requires antimalarial medications that target the parasite. Chikungunya treatment mainly focuses on relieving fever, managing joint pain, maintaining hydration, and ensuring adequate rest.

Parents should avoid giving aspirin or ibuprofen to children with suspected dengue unless specifically advised by a doctor, as these medications may increase the risk of bleeding. Paracetamol is generally considered safer for fever management when prescribed appropriately.

Preventing Mosquito-Borne Diseases in Children

Prevention remains the most effective way to protect children from dengue, malaria, and chikungunya. Parents should eliminate stagnant water around their homes, including water collected in buckets, flower pots, coolers, discarded tires, and containers where mosquitoes can breed.

Children should wear full-sleeved clothing, especially during mosquito-active hours. Sleeping under mosquito nets, using child-safe mosquito repellents, installing window screens, and keeping the surroundings clean can significantly reduce mosquito exposure. Schools and communities should also participate in mosquito control efforts by maintaining proper sanitation and preventing water stagnation.

Encouraging children to stay hydrated, eat a balanced diet, and seek medical attention promptly whenever fever develops during the monsoon season also supports early recovery and reduces the risk of complications.

When Should Parents Visit a Pediatrician?

Parents should never wait for symptoms to become severe before consulting a pediatrician. Any child with high fever lasting more than 24 to 48 hours during the monsoon season should undergo proper medical evaluation. Early diagnosis allows doctors to begin appropriate treatment before complications develop.

Even if the fever initially appears mild, continuous monitoring is important because conditions like dengue may worsen after the fever starts improving. Following your pediatrician's advice regarding hydration, medications, blood tests, and follow-up visits ensures the safest recovery for your child.

Final Thoughts

Dengue, malaria, and chikungunya are common mosquito-borne illnesses that affect thousands of children every year, particularly during the rainy season. Although these diseases often begin with similar symptoms, they require timely diagnosis and appropriate medical care to prevent serious complications.

Parents should remain alert for warning signs such as persistent high fever, severe abdominal pain, repeated vomiting, bleeding, breathing difficulties, dehydration, unusual sleepiness, or seizures. Prompt consultation with a pediatrician can lead to early diagnosis, effective treatment, and a faster recovery.

Protecting children from mosquito bites, maintaining a clean environment, and seeking medical attention at the first sign of illness are the best ways to safeguard your child's health during the monsoon season.

title: Why Vaccination Is Important for Children | A Parent's Guide by Dr. Mayur Kumar Goyal
heading: Why Vaccination Is Important for Your Child
content:
Why Vaccination Is Important for Your Child

Every parent wants to keep their child healthy and protected from serious illnesses. Good food, proper sleep, hygiene, and regular health checkups all play an important role in a child's health.

But there is one more important part of protecting your child—vaccination.

Vaccines help protect children from several serious infectious diseases. They prepare a child's immune system to recognize and fight certain infections before those infections can cause serious illness.

For parents, understanding why vaccines are given and following the recommended vaccination schedule can help provide important protection during the early and growing years of a child's life.

How Do Vaccines Protect Your Child?

Our immune system is the body's natural defense against infections.

When a virus or bacteria enters the body, the immune system tries to recognize it and fight against it.

The problem is that when the body comes across a serious infection for the first time, it may not be prepared to fight it quickly enough.

This is where vaccines help.

Vaccines train the immune system to recognize specific infections and prepare a protective response against them.

You can think of it as giving your child's immune system a practice session before it faces the real infection.

If the child is exposed to that infection later, their immune system is better prepared to respond.

My Child Is Healthy. Does My Child Still Need Vaccines?

This is a common question among parents.

A healthy diet, physical activity, proper sleep, and good hygiene certainly help children stay healthy. However, being generally healthy does not provide specific protection against every infectious disease.

A healthy child can still become infected with diseases such as measles, polio, whooping cough, or other vaccine-preventable infections if exposed.

Vaccines provide specific protection against specific diseases.

That is why a healthy lifestyle and vaccination should work together rather than being considered alternatives.

Why Is Vaccination So Important for Children?

1. Young Children Are More Vulnerable to Some Infections

A baby's immune system is still developing during the early years of life.

This can make babies and young children more vulnerable to certain infections and their complications.

Vaccination helps provide protection during these important early years.

This is also why many vaccines are recommended during infancy and early childhood rather than waiting until the child grows older.

2. Vaccines Help Prevent Serious Diseases

Vaccination has helped protect children from many potentially serious diseases.

Depending on the recommended immunization schedule, vaccines can provide protection against diseases such as:

  • Polio
  • Measles
  • Tetanus
  • Diphtheria
  • Pertussis (Whooping Cough)
  • Hepatitis B
  • Tuberculosis
  • Rotavirus
  • Pneumococcal Disease
  • Other vaccine-preventable infections

Some of these illnesses can cause serious complications, particularly in babies and young children.

Vaccination helps reduce the risk of developing these preventable diseases and their complications.

3. Vaccination Is Safer Than Getting the Disease

Some parents wonder whether it is better for a child to develop natural immunity after getting an infection.

The problem is simple: to develop immunity through natural infection, the child first has to get the disease, which may not always be mild.

Some infections can cause severe illness, hospitalization, or long-term complications before the child develops immunity.

Vaccination allows the immune system to build protection without requiring the child to experience the full risks of the actual disease.

4. Vaccines Also Help Protect Other Children

Vaccination does more than protect one child.

When more children in a community are appropriately vaccinated, infections have fewer opportunities to spread.

This is especially important for babies and children with weakened immune systems.

By keeping your child's vaccinations up to date, you help protect both your child and the wider community.

Why Does My Child Need So Many Vaccines?

Parents often wonder why babies need so many vaccines at a young age.

The vaccination schedule is carefully designed to provide protection when children are most vulnerable and when vaccines can produce the best immune response.

Some vaccines require multiple doses to provide stronger and longer-lasting protection.

Following the recommended schedule ensures timely protection.

Why Are Booster Doses Needed?

Some vaccines require booster doses because protection may decrease over time.

Booster doses strengthen the immune response and help maintain long-term protection.

Completing the full vaccination schedule is essential.

What If My Child Misses a Vaccine?

Missing a vaccination does not always mean the schedule must start again.

Your pediatrician can recommend a catch-up vaccination schedule after reviewing your child's vaccination records.

Always carry your child's vaccination card during consultations.

Can My Child Get Vaccinated If They Have a Cold or Fever?

A mild illness does not always require vaccination to be postponed.

However, parents should consult their pediatrician if the child has fever, cough, vomiting, diarrhea, allergies, previous vaccine reactions, chronic illness, or is taking regular medicines.

Are Mild Reactions After Vaccination Normal?

Some children may develop mild fever, redness, swelling at the injection site, irritability, tiredness, or reduced appetite after vaccination.

These reactions are usually temporary.

If severe symptoms such as breathing difficulty, facial swelling, seizures, or extreme weakness occur, seek immediate medical attention.

Keep Your Child's Vaccination Record Safe

Maintain your child's vaccination records carefully.

These records help your pediatrician identify completed vaccines, pending doses, and boosters.

Keeping a digital copy can also be helpful.

Vaccination Is Only One Part of Keeping Your Child Healthy

Vaccination works best along with healthy habits such as a balanced diet, adequate sleep, physical activity, good hygiene, clean drinking water, regular health checkups, and growth monitoring.

Have Questions About Your Child's Vaccination?

Parents often have questions about vaccine schedules, boosters, missed vaccines, and vaccination during illness.

Instead of relying on social media or incomplete online information, consult a qualified pediatrician.

Every child is different, and vaccination advice should always be personalized according to age, medical history, and previous vaccinations.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)

1. Why is vaccination important for children?

Vaccination protects children from serious infectious diseases and reduces the risk of complications.

2. Is vaccination necessary if my child is healthy?

Yes. Healthy children also need vaccines because a healthy lifestyle alone cannot prevent infectious diseases.

3. What should I do if my child has missed a vaccine?

Consult your pediatrician for a catch-up vaccination schedule.

4. Why do some vaccines require booster doses?

Booster doses strengthen and maintain immunity over time.

5. Can my child get vaccinated if they have a cold?

It depends on the child's condition. Always consult your pediatrician before vaccination.

6. Is fever normal after vaccination?

Mild fever and soreness are common temporary reactions after some vaccines.

7. Where can I get information about my child's vaccination schedule?

Your pediatrician can guide you according to your child's age and vaccination history.

8. Should I keep my child's old vaccination records?

Yes. Vaccination records help doctors determine completed and pending vaccines throughout childhood.
